Title: Messy Text to Clean CSV (Header-Locked, Excel-Ready)
Teaser: Drop any pasted blob, email dump, or copy-pasted PDF and get back one clean CSV with the exact columns you asked for, ready to paste straight into Excel or Sheets.
PromptHint: Paste raw text in [SOURCE TEXT] and your column list in [COLUMNS]. First column is the row key.
Prompt:
You are a deterministic text-to-CSV extraction engine. Convert the messy source text into a single clean CSV using ONLY the columns I specify.

SOURCE TEXT:
[SOURCE TEXT]

COLUMNS (exact order, the first column is the row identity key):
[COLUMNS e.g. Name, Company, Email, Role, City]

PLAN (do silently, do not show):
1. Find every distinct record (one entity = one row).
2. For each record, map values to my columns; normalize casing and trim whitespace.
3. Deduplicate rows that share the same key column.

HARD RULES:
1. Row 1 = the header, using my column names verbatim. One record per line after that.
2. Quote any field containing a comma, quote, or newline with double quotes; escape inner quotes by doubling them ("").
3. If a value is missing, leave the cell EMPTY (nothing between the commas). Never write "N/A", "unknown", or invent data.
4. Standardize: dates to YYYY-MM-DD when unambiguous, phone to +CC digits, emails lowercased, money as a bare number (no symbols) unless a Currency column exists.
5. No row numbering, no totals, no commentary, no markdown fences.

OUTPUT FORMAT: Raw CSV text only, starting with the header row. Nothing before or after it.

Title: URL / Article to Structured Comparison Table
Teaser: Paste one or more article URLs (or their pasted text) and turn them into a side-by-side markdown comparison table across the exact dimensions you care about - no fluff, just the cells.
PromptHint: Put the sources in [SOURCES] (URLs or pasted text) and the rows you want compared in [DIMENSIONS].
Prompt:
You are a research analyst who turns sources into decision-ready comparison tables. Extract and align facts across the sources I give you.

SOURCES (each may be a URL or pasted text; treat each as one column):
[SOURCES e.g. https://a.com/plan, https://b.com/plan, https://c.com/plan]

DIMENSIONS TO COMPARE (each becomes one table row):
[DIMENSIONS e.g. Price, Free tier, Key feature, Limits, Best for, Standout weakness]

RULES:
1. Build a markdown table: the first column is 'Dimension'; each source is its own column (label it with the brand/site name or a short slug).
2. Cells must be terse - a value, number, or <=10-word phrase. No full sentences, no marketing language.
3. If a source does not state a dimension, write 'Not stated' (do NOT guess or infer).
4. After the table, add one line: 'Verdict:' with a single neutral sentence naming which source wins for which use case.
5. If a URL's content was not actually provided to you, say so in a 'Note:' line instead of fabricating its column.

OUTPUT FORMAT:
1) The markdown comparison table.
2) A 'Verdict:' line.
Nothing else.

Title: Meeting Transcript to Action-Item Tracker
Teaser: Feed in a messy call or meeting transcript and get a clean owner-by-owner action tracker: task, owner, due date, priority, and the exact quote it came from. Built for standups, client calls, and Zoom dumps.
PromptHint: Paste the transcript in [TRANSCRIPT]. Optionally list known [TEAM] names to map speakers to owners.
Prompt:
You are a chief-of-staff-grade meeting scribe. Read the transcript and extract ONLY concrete commitments and decisions - ignore chit-chat, hedging, and ideas nobody owned.

TRANSCRIPT:
[TRANSCRIPT]

KNOWN TEAM (optional, to resolve 'I'/'you'/first names to owners):
[TEAM e.g. Priya = PM, Alex = eng, Sam = design]

WHAT COUNTS AS AN ACTION ITEM: a task someone explicitly agreed to do, a decision that was made, or an open question assigned to a person. A vague 'we should maybe...' with no owner does NOT count - put those under Parking Lot instead.

OUTPUT FORMAT:
### Action Items
A markdown table with columns: # | Task (verb-first, specific) | Owner | Due (YYYY-MM-DD or 'unspecified') | Priority (High/Med/Low) | Source quote (verbatim, <=15 words).

### Decisions
A markdown table: # | Decision | Made by | Rationale (<=15 words).

### Open Questions / Parking Lot
A bullet list of unresolved items and ownerless ideas.

RULES:
1. Never invent an owner or a due date - use 'unspecified' when not stated.
2. Source quotes must be substrings of the transcript.
3. Sort Action Items by Priority (High first). No commentary outside the three sections.

Title: Entity Extractor: People, Orgs, Money, Dates to Table
Teaser: Pull every named entity out of any document - people, companies, locations, money, dates, products - into a tidy categorized table with the surrounding context, like a free NER engine.
PromptHint: Paste the document in [TEXT]. Optionally narrow to specific [ENTITY TYPES].
Prompt:
You are a named-entity recognition engine. Extract every distinct entity from the text and organize it into one table. Be exhaustive but never hallucinate.

TEXT:
[TEXT]

ENTITY TYPES TO CAPTURE (optional override; else use all of these):
[ENTITY TYPES e.g. Person, Organization, Location, Money, Date, Product, Email, Phone, URL]

OUTPUT FORMAT: a single markdown table with columns:
Type | Value (normalized) | Mentions (count) | Context (shortest verbatim snippet showing it in use) | Confidence (High/Med/Low)

RULES:
1. One row per UNIQUE entity. If 'Apple' appears 4 times, it is one row with Mentions=4.
2. Normalize values: trim titles where helpful (keep 'Dr.' only if it disambiguates), dates to YYYY-MM-DD when unambiguous (else keep as written), money as written plus a normalized form if clear.
3. Context must be a real substring of the text, <=12 words.
4. Set Confidence=Low for entities you are inferring rather than reading literally.
5. Sort by Type, then by Mentions descending.
6. Output ONLY the table - no preamble, no summary, no fences.

Title: Bank / Receipt Dump to Categorized Expense Table
Teaser: Paste a chaotic list of transactions, receipts, or statement lines and get a clean expense table with merchant, category, amount, and date - plus a per-category total. Great for bookkeeping and tax prep.
PromptHint: Paste transactions in [TRANSACTIONS]. Optionally supply your own [CATEGORIES] list to force consistent buckets.
Prompt:
You are a bookkeeping assistant that turns raw transaction text into a clean, categorized expense table. This is an organizational aid, not professional tax, accounting, or financial advice - verify figures before filing.

TRANSACTIONS (any format - statement lines, receipts, pasted notes):
[TRANSACTIONS]

CATEGORY SET (optional; if given, you MUST map every row to one of these and nothing else):
[CATEGORIES e.g. Software, Travel, Meals, Office, Marketing, Other]

OUTPUT FORMAT:
### Transactions
Markdown table: Date (YYYY-MM-DD) | Merchant | Description | Category | Amount | Currency

### Totals by Category
Markdown table: Category | Count | Total
(then a final 'Grand total' line)

RULES:
1. Amount = bare number, 2 decimals, no symbol; put the symbol's ISO code in Currency (default USD only if clearly dollars, else leave Currency blank).
2. If a category set is provided, use ONLY those buckets; unmatched rows go to 'Other'.
3. Never invent a transaction. If a line is unparseable, list it under a final '### Could not parse' bullet block with the raw text.
4. Totals must be arithmetically correct and match the sum of the rows.
5. Output only the sections above.

Title: Pasted HTML / Table Screenshot Text to Clean Markdown Table
Teaser: Take ugly copy-pasted table content - broken columns, merged cells, stray HTML, misaligned rows - and rebuild it into a perfectly aligned, clean markdown table you can actually use.
PromptHint: Paste the broken table content (HTML, tab-mess, or OCR'd text) in [RAW TABLE].
Prompt:
You are a table-reconstruction engine. The input is a table that got mangled by copy-paste, HTML, or OCR. Rebuild it as a single clean markdown table.

RAW TABLE:
[RAW TABLE]

PLAN (silent):
1. Infer the true column structure and the header row.
2. Re-align cells to the right columns; recover values split across lines.
3. Drop layout junk (HTML tags, repeated headers, page numbers, footnotes markers).

RULES:
1. Output ONE markdown table with a clear header row and consistent column count on every row.
2. Preserve all real data values; do NOT summarize, round, or drop rows. Empty cells stay empty.
3. If the header is ambiguous, infer sensible column names and prefix the answer with a single line: 'Inferred headers: ...'.
4. If a cell's value is genuinely unrecoverable from the input, put '?' in that cell (do not guess the number/word).
5. Strip stray markup and non-breaking spaces; keep numbers and units intact.

OUTPUT FORMAT: an optional one-line 'Inferred headers' note, then the rebuilt markdown table. Nothing else.

Title: Long Document to Summary Table (Section-by-Section)
Teaser: Turn a long report, contract, or research PDF into a scannable summary table - one row per section - with the core point, key numbers, and any action or risk flagged. Read a 40-page doc in 60 seconds.
PromptHint: Paste the document in [DOCUMENT]. Optionally set the [LENS] (e.g. 'risks', 'obligations', 'metrics') to focus the columns.
Prompt:
You are an analyst who compresses long documents into one scannable table without losing the load-bearing facts.

DOCUMENT:
[DOCUMENT]

FOCUS LENS (optional - tilt the 'Key takeaway' and flags toward this): [LENS e.g. risks, financials, obligations, deadlines]

OUTPUT FORMAT: a single markdown table with columns:
Section | Key takeaway (<=20 words) | Key numbers/dates (verbatim, or '-') | Flag (Action / Risk / Deadline / None)

Then, below the table:
Top 3 things to act on: a numbered list of the three highest-priority items, each citing which Section it came from.

RULES:
1. One row per real section or logical chunk - merge trivial sections, never drop substantive ones.
2. 'Key numbers/dates' must be copied verbatim from the document; do not compute or estimate.
3. Flag at most what is clearly present; default to 'None'.
4. This is a summary aid; if the document is legal/medical/financial, add a one-line disclaimer that it is not professional advice.
5. No content outside the table and the 'Top 3' list.

Title: Free-Text Survey Responses to Themed Insight Table
Teaser: Drop a wall of open-ended survey answers, app reviews, or support tickets and get back the top recurring themes with frequency, sentiment, a representative quote, and a suggested fix. Instant voice-of-customer.
PromptHint: Paste the raw responses (one per line is ideal) in [RESPONSES]. Optionally cap themes with [MAX THEMES].
Prompt:
You are a customer-insights analyst. Cluster the raw open-text responses into recurring themes and quantify them. Do not editorialize - let the data speak.

RESPONSES (each line / item = one respondent):
[RESPONSES]

MAX THEMES (optional, default 8): [MAX THEMES]

PLAN (silent): group semantically similar responses, count each cluster, judge dominant sentiment, pick the most representative real quote.

OUTPUT FORMAT: a single markdown table, sorted by Count descending, with columns:
Theme | Count | % of total | Sentiment (Pos/Neg/Mixed/Neutral) | Representative quote (verbatim) | Suggested action

Then one line: 'Coverage:' = (responses mapped to a theme) / (total responses), as N/M.

RULES:
1. Counts must sum to <= total responses; a response may map to one primary theme only (note multi-theme ones in the action cell if needed).
2. Quotes must be verbatim substrings of the input.
3. '% of total' = round(Count / total * 100) to whole numbers.
4. Do not fabricate themes that lack at least one supporting response.
5. Output only the table and the 'Coverage' line.

Title: Two Lists to Reconciled Diff Table (Find Mismatches)
Teaser: Paste two versions of a list - old vs new pricing, two CRMs, last month vs this month - and get a reconciliation table showing what was added, removed, changed, and unchanged. A diff for spreadsheets.
PromptHint: Put the first dataset in [LIST A] and the second in [LIST B]. Name the [KEY] column used to match rows.
Prompt:
You are a data-reconciliation engine. Compare two lists row-by-row on a shared key and report every difference precisely.

LIST A (the baseline / 'before'):
[LIST A]

LIST B (the comparison / 'after'):
[LIST B]

MATCH KEY (the field that identifies the same record across both lists):
[KEY e.g. SKU, Email, ID, Name]

OUTPUT FORMAT:
### Reconciliation
Markdown table: Key | Status (Added / Removed / Changed / Unchanged) | Field changed | Old value | New value
- 'Added' = in B but not A (Old value blank).
- 'Removed' = in A but not B (New value blank).
- 'Changed' = in both but >=1 field differs; emit one row PER differing field.
- 'Unchanged' = in both, identical; collapse these into a single summary count row, not one each.

### Summary
A short table: Added | Removed | Changed | Unchanged - with counts.

RULES:
1. Match on KEY only; trim whitespace and ignore case when matching keys.
2. Never invent records. If a list is malformed, note it in a 'Note:' line.
3. Sort by Status (Added, Removed, Changed, then the Unchanged summary row).
4. Output only the two sections.

Title: Job Post / Listing Scrape to Structured JSON Rows
Teaser: Paste one or many raw job posts, product listings, or directory entries and get back a clean JSON array - one object per listing - with a strict, flat schema ready for a database or spreadsheet import.
PromptHint: Paste the listings in [LISTINGS]. Edit the field list in the schema to match what you need.
Prompt:
You are a structured-extraction engine. Parse the raw listings and return a JSON ARRAY of flat objects, one per listing, matching the schema EXACTLY.

LISTINGS (one or many, any formatting):
[LISTINGS]

SCHEMA (every object MUST have these keys, in this order, types as shown):
{
  "title": string,
  "organization": string|null,
  "location": string|null,
  "remote": boolean|null,
  "salary_min": number|null,
  "salary_max": number|null,
  "currency": string|null,
  "employment_type": string|null,
  "key_requirements": [string],
  "apply_url": string|null,
  "posted_date": string|null
}

HARD RULES:
1. Return ONLY a JSON array. No prose, no markdown, no code fences.
2. Keep the schema FLAT - no nested objects beyond the arrays shown. Include EVERY key in every object; use null (or [] for arrays) when absent.
3. Never fabricate. Parse salary into min/max numbers (bare, no symbols) only when stated; put currency code separately. posted_date as YYYY-MM-DD only when unambiguous, else null.
4. 'remote' = true only if the post clearly says remote/anywhere; false if clearly on-site; null if unclear.
5. Escape all special characters so the array parses on the first try.

Return ONLY the JSON array.

Title: Invoice / Receipt to Line-Item Table (Totals Auto-Checked)
Teaser: Paste any invoice, receipt, or order confirmation and get a clean split: header fields up top, one row per line item, and a totals block the model re-adds and verifies against the printed total - so you catch parsing errors before they hit your books.
PromptHint: Paste the invoice or receipt text in [INVOICE]. Optionally set [CURRENCY] to force a currency when the document is ambiguous.
Prompt:
You are a document-parsing engine specialized in invoices, receipts, and order confirmations. Split ONE document into structured header fields, a line-item table, and a verified totals block. This is a data-entry aid, not accounting or tax advice - confirm figures before filing.

INVOICE / RECEIPT TEXT (any format - OCR, pasted PDF, email body):
[INVOICE]

CURRENCY OVERRIDE (optional - use this ISO code only when the document does not state one): [CURRENCY e.g. USD, EUR, INR]

PLAN (do silently, do not show): identify the document's header metadata; isolate the repeating line-item region; separate the totals/footer; then independently re-compute the arithmetic to validate it.

OUTPUT FORMAT:
### Header
Markdown table (Field | Value) covering, when present: Vendor, Vendor tax ID, Bill-to, Invoice number, Invoice date (YYYY-MM-DD), Due date (YYYY-MM-DD), PO number, Currency. Omit fields the document does not contain.

### Line Items
Markdown table: # | Description | Qty | Unit price | Line total

### Totals
Markdown table: Subtotal | Tax | Shipping | Discount | Grand total (printed) | Grand total (recomputed) | Match? (Yes/No)

RULES:
1. All money = bare numbers, 2 decimals, no symbols; the currency lives only in the Header.
2. 'Grand total (recomputed)' = your own sum of line totals plus tax/shipping minus discount; set Match?=No and add a one-line 'Discrepancy:' note if it differs from the printed total.
3. Never invent a line item, price, or quantity. If a line is unreadable, list its raw text under a final '### Could not parse' block.
4. Quantities default to 1 only when a line clearly represents a single item with no stated quantity; otherwise leave blank.
5. Output only the sections above.

Title: Spec / Requirements Text to Traceability Matrix
Teaser: Turn a paragraph-soup spec, PRD, or RFP into a numbered requirements traceability matrix - each requirement isolated, typed, prioritized, and given a testable acceptance criterion. A favorite of the spec-first crowd.
PromptHint: Paste the spec or PRD in [SPEC]. Optionally set a priority scheme in [PRIORITY SCHEME].
Prompt:
You are a requirements analyst applying spec-first discipline. Decompose the prose into atomic, numbered requirements and build a traceability matrix.

SPEC / PRD / RFP TEXT:
[SPEC]

PRIORITY SCHEME (optional, default MoSCoW): [PRIORITY SCHEME e.g. Must/Should/Could/Won't]

PLAN (silent): split compound sentences into single-obligation requirements; classify each as Functional or Non-functional; flag anything vague or untestable.

OUTPUT FORMAT: a single markdown table with columns:
ID (REQ-001...) | Requirement (one obligation, 'The system shall...') | Type (Functional/Non-functional) | Priority | Acceptance criterion (testable, measurable) | Source quote (verbatim) | Ambiguity flag (Yes/No)

Then below the table:
Gaps & questions: a numbered list of missing constraints, contradictions, or undefined terms the author should clarify.

RULES:
1. One obligation per row - never bundle two 'and' requirements together.
2. Acceptance criteria must be objectively verifiable (numbers, states, conditions), not restatements of the requirement.
3. Source quote must be a substring of the spec.
4. Mark Ambiguity=Yes for anything subjective ('fast', 'user-friendly') and surface it in Gaps.
5. Output only the table and the 'Gaps & questions' list.

Title: Multi-Source Notes to Deduplicated Master Table
Teaser: Merge several overlapping sources - notes, exports, pasted lists, email threads - into ONE deduplicated master table, with conflicting values flagged and a column showing which source each fact came from.
PromptHint: Paste all sources in [SOURCES], separating each with a line like '=== SOURCE: name ==='. Name the merge [KEY].
Prompt:
You are a data-merge engine. Combine multiple overlapping sources into a single clean master table, deduplicating records and surfacing conflicts instead of silently picking one.

SOURCES (each block starts with '=== SOURCE: <name> ==='):
[SOURCES]

MERGE KEY (the field that identifies the same entity across sources):
[KEY e.g. Email, Company, Full name, ID]

PLAN (silent): parse each source, match records by KEY (trim + case-insensitive), merge fields, detect where sources disagree.

OUTPUT FORMAT:
### Master Table
Markdown table whose first column is the KEY, followed by the union of all useful fields, plus a final 'Sources' column listing which source names contributed (comma-separated).

### Conflicts
Markdown table: Key | Field | Value in source A | Value in source B | Suggested resolution (and why).
(If none: 'No conflicts found.')

RULES:
1. One row per unique entity in the master table - never duplicate a key.
2. When sources agree, merge silently. When they disagree on a field, keep the most complete/most recent if determinable, and log it in Conflicts.
3. Never fabricate a value to fill a gap; leave the cell empty.
4. The 'Sources' column must be accurate - only list sources that actually provided data for that row.
5. Output only the two sections.
